content.body: This document exists to define how App Behavior corpuses represent what exists today separately from what should govern implementation. Use it when auditing existing features, creating desired specs, or preparing a refactor from current behavior toward intended behavior.

content.title: Current UX
body: Current UX captures what users experience today. It may come from runtime observation, existing docs, user reports, or prior behavior records. Distinguish documented current from observed current: documented current is what App Behavior docs previously said exists, while observed current is what runtime investigation, tests, or source traversal show now.
Current UX is not automatically governing. It may be preserved, clarified, changed, removed, marked accidental, or left unresolved.
Use current UX to answer:
- What happens now?
- Which journeys and flows exist?
- Which behavior appears inconsistent or missing?
- Which expected basics are absent?
- How confident are we in the observation?

content.title: Behavior Status Alignment
body: Use the root behavior status vocabulary when current/desired separation is part of roadmap, implementation, proof, or closeout work.
`observed_runtime` maps to current evidence, not automatic governing intent.
`documented_current` maps to current behavior already captured in App Behavior.
`verified_current` maps to current behavior reconciled and proven for the relevant scope.
`intended_target` and `intended_change` map to desired/target behavior only when authority and proof requirements are explicit.
`ideal_future` and `out_of_scope_candidate` may be documented as future or non-current behavior, but they must not silently expand current implementation scope.
`runtime_drift` and `pending_decision` must be routed before implementation readiness if they affect the current scope.

content.title: Desired UX
body: Desired UX captures what should govern implementation.
Use desired UX to answer:
- What should the user experience be?
- Which behaviors are confirmed?
- Which changes are approved?
- Which behavior is pending confirmation?
- What must implementation and tests prove?

content.title: Same Schema, Different Mode
body: Current and desired requirements use the same `uxRequirement` shape. The distinction is the `mode`, `status`, `authority`, and mapping relationship.
Current:
```json
"mode": "current",
"authority": "observed-current"
Desired:
```json
"mode": "desired",
"authority": "governing"

content.title: Decision Boundary
body: Implementation should be built against desired governing UX. Current UX should inform desired UX, but it should not silently become the implementation target.
When current and desired differ, a current-to-desired mapping must explain the decision, recommendation, confirmation status, and implementation gate.

content.title: Central Row Lifecycle Alignment
body: Current and desired separation must preserve central App Behavior rows as the behavior authority.
Planning corpuses may include `central_app_behavior_row_snapshot` records only as non-governing cached full-row copies for orientation.

New observed current behavior, newly specified desired behavior, runtime drift, and replaced old behavior must update central rows or enter the corpus-wide `behavior_row_reconciliation_ledger` until applied, bounded, future-scoped, or blocked.
Audit, PR review, verification, proof, and closeout must use central rows directly, not cached snapshots.
